title: Bangladesh Finance Ministry Unveils Historic Tk 9.2 Trillion Budget Framework
summary: The proposed budget prioritizes digital infrastructure and rural electrification, marking the largest capital investment allocation in the nation's history.
content:
Finance Minister Abul Hassan Mahmud Ali presented the outlines of Bangladesh's proposed national budget for the fiscal year 2026–27 on Monday, revealing a record Tk 9.2 trillion framework that prioritizes digital infrastructure, rural electrification, and social safety net expansion.

The proposed budget represents a 14.3 percent increase over the revised estimates for the current fiscal year and allocates approximately Tk 2.7 trillion toward development expenditure — the largest such allocation in the country's history.

"This budget is a statement of national ambition," the Finance Minister said during a press briefing at the secretariat. "We are investing in the foundations of a prosperous Bangladesh that our children will inherit."

Among the headline proposals, Tk 420 billion has been earmarked for the expansion of the national power grid to reach the last 3.4 percent of households still without electricity. An additional Tk 310 billion will fund the rollout of high-speed fiber internet to all upazila headquarters by December 2027.

The social protection sector will receive Tk 1.18 trillion, covering expanded allowances for widows, the elderly, and persons with disabilities, as well as a new conditional cash transfer program for rural school enrollment.

Economists have offered mixed reactions. While many praise the ambition of the investment targets, some analysts have raised concerns about the projected fiscal deficit of 5.2 percent of GDP and the government's ability to mobilize the required revenue through the National Board of Revenue, which fell short of its collection target by Tk 340 billion in the current fiscal year.

Opposition parties have called the budget "election-motivated" given the upcoming municipal cycle, while business groups have broadly welcomed the infrastructure spending commitments. The full budget proposal will be submitted to the National Parliament on June 2, 2026.

title: Dhaka Rapid Transit Line 6 Reaches 500,000 Daily Rider Milestone
summary: Bangladesh's flagship urban rail project exceeds ridership projections six months ahead of schedule, prompting calls to accelerate Line 2 construction.
content:
The Dhaka Mass Rapid Transit Line 6 recorded its 500,000th daily rider on Friday, surpassing projections set at the project's inauguration by a margin that has surprised even its most optimistic planners. The milestone was reached six months ahead of the schedule outlined in the original ridership forecast submitted to the Asian Development Bank.

Dhaka North City Corporation Mayor Atiqul Islam hailed the achievement as proof that Dhaka's commuters were ready to embrace modern public transport. "This is not just a number — it is a statement about how our city is changing," he said at a brief ceremony at the Uttara North station.

The success of Line 6 has intensified pressure on authorities to fast-track construction of Line 2, which will connect the Gabtoli bus terminal to the Narayanganj satellite town via the city center. Currently in the detailed design phase, Line 2 was originally slated to begin construction in late 2027, but transport ministry officials acknowledged Monday that the timeline is under review.

Commuters interviewed at several stations cited dramatic reductions in travel time as the primary reason for switching from buses and rickshaws. A journey from Uttara to Agargaon that previously consumed up to 90 minutes during peak hours now takes approximately 16 minutes aboard the MRT.

However, infrastructure analysts have pointed to persistent overcrowding at interchange stations as a challenge that must be addressed before ridership grows further. The Dhaka Transport Coordination Authority has announced a feasibility study for platform extensions at three of the most congested stops.

title: Anti-Corruption Commission Files Charges Against 14 LGRD Officials
summary: The sweeping crackdown targets a procurement scandal involving ghost infrastructure projects worth nearly Tk 800 crore across four southern districts.
content:
The Anti-Corruption Commission filed formal charges on Thursday against fourteen officials of the Local Government and Rural Development Ministry in connection with a procurement scandal that investigators say involved the fabrication of infrastructure projects across four southern districts over a three-year period.

According to the charge sheet filed at the Dhaka Special Judge's Court, the accused allegedly conspired to approve payments for road construction, culvert installation, and community centre renovation projects that were either never commenced or completed at a fraction of the reported cost. The total value of the alleged fraud is estimated at Tk 798 crore.

The case, which has been under investigation since late 2024, emerged from a routine audit conducted by the Comptroller and Auditor General's office, which flagged discrepancies between budget disbursements and physical project completion rates in Barisal, Patuakhali, Bhola, and Borguna districts.

ACC Chairman Mohammad Moinuddin Abdullah said the charges reflected the Commission's commitment to accountability at every level of government. "No rank offers immunity from the law," he told reporters outside the Commission's headquarters. "We will pursue this case to its conclusion."

Defence attorneys for several of the accused have indicated they will challenge the charges, arguing that the audit discrepancies resulted from documentation failures rather than intentional fraud. Bail hearings are scheduled for next week.

title: Bangladesh Bank Raises Policy Rate to 9.5% to Curb Persistent Inflation
summary: The central bank's monetary policy committee voted unanimously to tighten borrowing conditions as consumer prices remain elevated for the eighth consecutive month.
content:
Bangladesh Bank raised its benchmark policy rate by 50 basis points to 9.5 percent on Wednesday, marking the third upward adjustment in twelve months as the central bank continues its campaign to bring consumer price inflation back within its 6 percent target band.

The Monetary Policy Committee voted unanimously in favor of the increase, citing persistent price pressures in the food and energy segments that have kept headline inflation above 9 percent for eight consecutive months. The committee also noted that global commodity prices, while easing from their recent peaks, remain elevated relative to pre-pandemic baselines.

Governor Ahsan H. Mansur acknowledged the difficulty of tightening monetary conditions in an economy where a large proportion of households lack access to formal credit but said the long-term costs of entrenched inflation would be more damaging than the short-term pain of higher borrowing costs.

The banking sector responded to the announcement with a broad rise in lending rates, with several major commercial banks issuing revised rate schedules within hours of the central bank's statement. Mortgage rates at the state-owned Sonali Bank are now expected to cross 11 percent, their highest level in over a decade.

Business groups expressed concern about the impact on investment, particularly in the small and medium enterprise sector, which relies heavily on working capital loans at floating rates. The Federation of Bangladesh Chambers of Commerce and Industry called for targeted credit support programs to offset the tightening effect on growth.

title: Sylhet Flash Floods Displace Over 120,000 Residents in 48 Hours
summary: Unusually heavy pre-monsoon rainfall triggers rapid flooding across six upazilas, with relief operations hampered by damaged road infrastructure.
content:
Flash floods triggered by exceptionally heavy pre-monsoon rainfall inundated low-lying areas across six upazilas in the Sylhet division over the weekend, displacing an estimated 120,000 residents and prompting the activation of emergency relief protocols by the Disaster Management and Relief Ministry.

The Bangladesh Meteorological Department recorded over 340 millimetres of rainfall at Sylhet station in a 48-hour window ending Saturday evening — more than triple the monthly average for March and the highest recorded total for the period since records began in 1965.

District administration officials said the speed of the flooding caught many communities off guard, with water levels rising several feet in under six hours in some low-lying haor areas. Dozens of villages in Sunamganj and Netrokona districts were completely submerged, with residents evacuating by boat to higher ground or school buildings designated as temporary shelters.

Relief operations have been complicated by damage to road infrastructure, with at least 47 kilometres of district and upazila roads reported as impassable due to waterlogging or embankment collapse. The Bangladesh Army has deployed engineering units to assist with access route restoration.

The Ministry of Disaster Management and Relief has released Tk 15 crore in emergency funds and dispatched 500 metric tonnes of dry food rations to the affected areas. Aid workers warned that the risk of waterborne disease outbreaks would grow significantly if clean water access was not restored within 72 hours.
